speaker: interviewer
text: January the 8th, 1996. The survivor is Joseph Carver, born Schnitzer. I'm Susan Fransman in London, England, and the language is English. I'm Susan Fransman. The date today is the 8th of February, 1996. I'm interviewing Joseph Carver in London, England, and the language of the interview is in English. Would you tell me your name, and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Carver, spelled C-A-R-V-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schnitzer. Spelled S-C-H-N-I-T-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My age today is 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city where you were born. And would you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leipzig, Germany. And it's spelled L-E-I-P-Z-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you start by telling me your earliest memory as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My childhood, which started when I was five years old-- and that was about the time when Hitler came to power, in 1933. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Straight away, we found that the anti-Semitism of the German population came out of the blue. Well, there was some anti-Semitism all the time, but the people who were Social Democrats and Communists changed shirts overnight. The red shirts became the brown shirts. People who used to eat at our table, friends of ours, suddenly disappeared, put on Nazi uniforms, and shunned us. We were non-existent to them. We lived in Germany, in Leipzig, at number 10 Huttenstrasse. And my grandmother, who was a very wealthy widow, lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an apartment block. We lived on the third floor. And on the first floor was another Jewish family living. They had two boys. We went to the same school. Now, all these people, all these Germans who were very friendly with us before Hitler, suddenly shunned us completely. We were non-existent. They didn't wanted to be seen talking to us. We found that it was getting worse and worse. We were not allowed to use the tram cars. We were not allowed to go swimming, to the swimming baths. We were not allowed to go to the parks. In fact, we were not allowed to go to all the other places of enjoyment, which normal children had.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child, what did you make of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I was very confused. Then it was explained to me why these things were done to us. I went to Hebrew classes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Joe, sorry. Can you just tell me what you were told?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told that we are going through very hard times where we are being persecuted as Jews on account of our religion. And I was attending Hebrew classes at the time, and our teachers, the rabbis, explained to us how, from days immemorial, so to speak, the persecution of Jews had started since the destruction of the Second Temple and events throughout the Middle Ages. And we had it in the 18th century, in Romania and Russia and Poland and in Germany, even before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So slowly, slowly, we got used to it. It became necessary for me to enter a school. And my mother, who was blonde and had blue eyes-- didn't look Jewish at all-- took me one-- to one of the nearest schools. The headmaster, seeing a very good-looking woman, said, yes, Mrs. Schnitzer, do sit down. So this is the little fellow. How old are you? I said, five and a half. Oh, that's splendid. Can you read? Yes. Can you write? Yes. Write your name. Oh, jolly good. That's nice. And do you know your sums? And they asked me the sums, and how much is two and two and six and eight and so on. Then he said, What is your name? And I said, Zalel. And he said to my mother, I never heard of this name. My mother said, it's a biblical name. It's from the Bible. Oh, he said. That's what it is. All right. Then he said, aren't you by any chance Jewish? He said to me, not to my mother. And I said, yes. I am Jewish. So he turned to my mother, and he said, dearest lady. We are not allowed to take Jewish children. If this is your son, maybe, you see, you've married a Jew. Even then, we couldn't consider it. I'm very sorry, lady. And my mother got up, and out we went. So this wasn't a fruitful outing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day we went to another school. There, the headmaster was in full uniform, strutting around like a peacock, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was in uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: The headmaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what uniform was he wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Nazi uniform of the stormtroopers. A brown uniform, he wore, with his belt and insignia. And he said to my mother, yes, I think we will be able to take him. Thought it was nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he turned around and he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, by the way, one minute, he says. Would you like to sign the certificate of genealogy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like grandparents, great-grandparents, and religion and all this? So when my mother put down Jewish and all this, he tore it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very angry. And he said, out, Jews, out! He says, don't you dare come into my German school. So out we went. Couldn't go to a Jewish school, because the Jewish school was confiscated by the German government. It was a very imposing building, and we were not allowed to have our scholastic education there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the education was carried out in some rented rooms owned by Jewish landlords in a different part of the city. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they interviewed me and my mother-- my father as well-- and they accepted me. But because I already could read and write and do sums, they put me in a higher class. How many other children were at that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that school there were, oh, quite a few, because some of my teachers I met in England after the war. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't meet any school friends after the war. But schoolmasters I did meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But approximately, how many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, in my class, we were about 20, 22, 20, 22 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. So this carried on for about a few months. I'm talking now about 1933-- '34-- '34, '35. '34, '35. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the Jewish school, which was confiscated by the Nazi, was given back for a short period, and we went to that Jewish school which had large classrooms and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] good equipment for gymnastics. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was almost just like it was before. They didn't really touch anything. So here we spend, I would think, about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever explained why it was given back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it just happened. They gave it back. Then they confiscated the school again, and we were sent to a school called the Catholic School for-- Catholic School for Citizens. Something like that. The Germans got such fancy names for everything, you see, and it sounds like a glorified thing in German. But when you translate it into English, it's nothing, really. It's a Catholic school. And that Catholic school was primarily for people of mixed religions.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean mixed marriages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed marriages, like Catholics. Mainly Catholics with Jews or Jews with Catholics, vice-versa, either women or men. And in my class, the teachers were also of mixed marriages. They were half-Jews, what we called half-Jews. They had Jewish-sounding names, like Goldstone, Silverstone, or Silverman, or something like that. But they wore their cross with pride. They still didn't believe that they were singled out to be Jewish. As far as they were concerned, they were Catholics. My school friends, they never saw that they were Jewish. They were Catholic. They were Germans. And we started to be persecuted by them as well, which really wasn't good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you persecuted by them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they taunted us, and they said Jew boy and Jew pig and all this. Now, I am usually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very much a sort of a pacifist. But when I am aroused in anger, then I really go mad. And one of these boys taunted me, and I got hold of him. And I knocked his head in the wall. And I said, all right. That's for calling me a Jew. And you are a Jew yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, many teachers came, and-- to find out what it was all about. And I told them. I said, he called me a dirty Jew. Jew boy. And he's a Jew himself. So they asked the boy to apologize to me, and they said this will never happen again. And this was in 1938, just before we were deported to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish life in Leipzig at the time, in 1938, before the deportation, was very restrictive. But Jews could still practice their religion. So Saturday morning was normal service in the synagogue. And people used to come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about the synagogues?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue were very beautiful. Our synagogue was the largest one in Saxony. It had a seating capacity of 1,500 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was built by one of the great philanthropists, a man called Isaac Israel Eitingon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was a great benefactor for Jewish causes. He endowed a Jewish hospital in his name, which was staffed by Jewish doctors, Jewish nurses, had a Jewish matron. And most of the people who went to this hospital were Jews. It was-- it has a good maternity clinic. My mother was there a couple of times as well, as far as I can remember. I was told. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had Jewish butchers. When the Germans were forbidding the shechita laws, we were not anymore able to attain Jewish-killed meat or chickens. And these were sent in from Denmark. And sometimes we used to get the supply from Poland. But slowly, slowly, this dried up as well. And then when we were deported, there was nothing at all, as far as Jewish meat and poultry was concerned. Before you were deported, how did you celebrate the festival, Shabbat, at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Shabbat was normal. We had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just like a normal Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had challah bread. We had fish. We had chicken, mainly chicken. Not so much meat, because meat became scarce. But chicken we had. My mother made some beautiful lokshen soup. And we had chopped liver, which was homemade. Everything was homemade. My mother used to bake and cook and everything. And it was a real delight to eat at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you light the candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had Shabbas candle, yes. But this was [LAUGHS] Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On Shabbat, of course, you see, you can't light candles. You can only light it the night before. So we had a nice, traditional Jewish life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke German. My father, who was born in Poland, came to Germany as a young man. He was about 19 or 20 at the time, which was just after the First World War-- 1914, '18. My mother was already living in Germany. Her parents came from Austria, which was called a part of Galicia, which is the Ukraine today. And she grew up in Germany. She went to school in Germany. And my parents married in March, 1920. We were four brothers at home. My brother Max, who is living in Florida-- he's alive. Then there was my brother Isidor. He died in the ghetto. There was me, myself. And a younger brother, called Ralph. He was born in 1935. He died-- well, he didn't die, actually. He was taken to an extermination camp with my mother. He was only seven years old at that time. So we were four boys at home. And although we were persecuted and we couldn't do anything, we couldn't go anywhere-- life was restricted, school-wise. And when we came home from school-- one incident I remember-- it was in 1937, I think. We came out from school, and we walked along-- you see, we never used any tram cars in Germany. So from home to go to school was about just under an hour. We walked it. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not allowed to use the tram cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So we walked through the park and then a long street and another street till we came to our school. So on our way back, we went to the park and saw a lot of people. And we couldn't get out of the park. It was shut. And there were a lot of stormtroopers there, and we got frightened. And they told us that Hitler was going to pass by very, very shortly. Staying at a hotel called the Kaiserhof.

speaker: interviewee
text: So like other people who were in the park, they were looking out, and me and my friend-- his name was Rolf Gronet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His parents were Romanian Jews. So we were watched, and the car approached. There were three cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: One car were the German dignitaries. Hitler's car was in the middle. He was there with another official. They told us later that this was the mayor of the city of Leipzig. And then there was another car. And after that, there were a lot of stormtroopers running around and making such a lot of racket. And that was outside the hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once Hitler was inside, they opened the park gates, and we could go out. And from the park to my house was about five minutes' walk. So at this point my friend Gronet, he came to us, and we told my mother, you know, that we'd seen Hitler. And we cursed him, [LAUGHS] you know. What did you do? How did you curse him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we said that the ground he's standing should swallow him up. Because he caused us such a lot of miseries. Little did we know that he would follow us all the time. So we had tea on that particular day. And after that, we took our bicycles-- I had my bicycle, and this Rolf, he used his bicycle at my place. And we used to cycle to his place. And we used to play around. And across the road where he lived, there was a very big shop called Hollander's. It was a theatrical agency. True to the name, Hollander, he was a Jewish man of Dutch origin. That's why he was called Hollander. And he had this theatrical agency. And as young boys, we were very curious what type of people would go inside and buy these costumes or hire these costumes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we became very friendly with the proprietor, who told us that, among these artists who came inside, there were some Jews or some even half-Jews. And some people who didn't want to talk about their ancestry was presumed to be Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found it rather fascinating. And we became like detectives, trying to find out who were these actresses, how they were Jewish, whether the grandmother was Jewish, or the great-grandmother? And we became quite experts on the racial laws which were predominant at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So life sort of carried on fairly normal, till we were deported to Poland. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was on the 28th of October, 1938. I usually used to leave my house 8 o'clock in the morning. My mother got up, gave me something to eat, and left me something to take back to school for my lunch. And we had a little cuckoo clock in our corridor. When it chimed 8:00, I would leave the house, kiss my mother goodbye, and go to school. We lived on the second floor. As I went down, I saw four people coming inside our block. It was an apartment building which went round, like in a half-circle. And two fellows went on the first floor as I was coming down. They were-- they knocked on the door, and they shouted, open up, in the name of the law!

speaker: interviewer
text: What uniform were they wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No uniforms. They were in civvies. But I could see that they were Gestapo. Because we already knew. We could smell them. Then, as they went up on the second floor, instead going to school, I had a premonition that something was wrong. I turned round and followed them. They knocked on my door, shouted the same thing-- open up, in the name of the law! My mother opened up. They stormed inside. I followed them. My, my father was still in bed. And my brothers were at home. And they went through all these, our property. And my father said, here's my passport. I'm a Polish citizen. You've got no right to be here. They say, shut up, you Jew boy. Be quiet. You can use this as your lavatory paper, Polish passport. This is our country. This is Germany. You get up. Get dressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he got dressed. We were not allowed to take anything whatsoever-- no documents, no photographs, no paper, nothing. We were not even allowed to eat anything. Just as we stood-- so my mother said to one of these Gestapo fellows, please, be a little bit merciful. I mean, these children need some clothing, and all this. So he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm not seeing. I am not seeing. So my mother took a few things, put it in a [INAUDIBLE]. And we left. As we came down, all the neighbors opened up the windows, looked down, and they spat. And they called us abusive names.

speaker: interviewee
text: We boarded the police car. In German, they call it [GERMAN], which means in, in English, like, Black Maria. So we were taken to the police station. And in the police station, we were interviewed, one by one, by higher ups in the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were asked our names, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we were born, where we were born, what nationality we had. In my case, they wanted to know what school I attended. Was it a Jewish school? And we, in those days, had a sort of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] understanding. Like, if the school was a Catholic school, we said it was a Catholic school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, how can Jews go to a Catholic school? Are you Catholic now? I said, no. The school is Catholic. Oh. Why don't you go to a Jewish school? I said, because they closed the Jewish school. But there aren't Jewish children going to that school. And there are children of mixed marriages going there. Oh, they said. That is a Jewish school. Just as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Germans, as I said before, they've got these special names. Like saying this is a Jewish school sounds all right in German, but when you translate it, it's sort of a derogatory. It means it's sort of a, a school for little Jewish pigs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother said to one of the police inspectors, we haven't eaten anything. The children are hungry. So he said, lady, take my sandwiches. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But don't show it to anybody. And that was that. So we had some sandwiches.
